mainTopic: (continues) משך זמן ההכרזה על אבידה · הכרזה על אבידה ודין הרמאי · טיפול באבידה ואחריות המוצא
overview: (1) The Gemara discusses the duration of the announcement period for lost items, analyzing the dispute between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yehuda and the impact of the destruction of the Temple and the rise of bandits. (2) The Gemara clarifies whether one announces 'lost item' or 'garment', and discusses the laws regarding a fraudulent claimant (רמאי) who must bring witnesses to prove his honesty. (3) The Gemara discusses the laws of maintaining lost animals, the duration of care required, and the dispute between Rabbi Tarfon and Rabbi Akiva regarding the finder's liability for loss. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara discusses the duration of the announcement period for lost items, analyzing the dispute between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yehuda and the impact of the destruction of the Temple and the rise of bandits.
The Gemara clarifies whether one announces 'lost item' or 'garment', and discusses the laws regarding a fraudulent claimant (רמאי) who must bring witnesses to prove his honesty.

The Gemara discusses the laws of maintaining lost animals, the duration of care required, and the dispute between Rabbi Tarfon and Rabbi Akiva regarding the finder's liability for loss. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Challenge regarding confusion with the first festival
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the previous answer by asking why the second festival is not also omitted to avoid confusion with the first.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a logical objection to the previous answer. This is a classic קושיא testing the consistency of the rule.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the concern is auditory confusion, why not omit the number on the second festival as well? An owner might mishear 'second' as 'first' and think there is still time. This challenges the consistency of the rule.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge applies the same logic of confusion to the second festival.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is testing the limits of the 'confusion' argument.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces a distinction between the consequences of confusion on the second vs. third festival.

phrases.english: because perhaps one who hears him will come to confuse it with the first pilgrimage Festival?

The Gemara answers: Confusing the second Festival with the first is not a problem, as in any case, won’t the finder come on the third pilgrimage Festival, thereby giving the owner another opportunity to recover his lost item?

rashi.hebrew: (אתי לאחלופי בשני - שמא יש שהוא סבור שאמר רגל שני:)
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern of confusion (אתי לאחלופי) applies specifically to the second festival, where one might mistakenly hear 'second' (שני) as 'first' (ראשון).

steps.title: The Baraita on the evolution of proclamation practices
steps.stepSummary: A Baraita describes how the proclamation practice evolved from the Temple period to the post-destruction era and the time of bandits.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces a ברייתא detailing the historical changes in the proclamation obligation. This is a setup line providing background context.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא states that initially, the proclamation lasted for three festivals and seven days. After the Temple's destruction, the Sages instituted proclamation in synagogues and study halls. Later, due to the rise of bandits, the Sages instituted that informing neighbors and acquaintances suffices.
steps.whatToRemember: The practice evolved from a long public proclamation to a shorter, more private notification due to changing circumstances.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'אַנָּסִים' refers to oppressive rulers or bandits who would confiscate found items.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shows the adaptability of halachic institutions to historical realities.

phrases.english: The Sages taught: Initially,

phrases.aramaic: כׇּל מִי שֶׁמָּצָא אֲבֵידָה הָיָה מַכְרִיז
phrases.english: anyone who found a lost item would proclaim
for three pilgrimage Festivals,
and for seven days after the last of the three pilgrimage Festivals,

phrases.aramaic: כְּדֵי שֶׁיֵּלֵךְ שְׁלֹשָׁה וְיַחְזוֹר שְׁלֹשָׁה וְיַכְרִיז יוֹם אֶחָד.
phrases.english: so that its owner will go to his home, a trip lasting up to three days, and will return to Jerusalem, a trip lasting up to three days, and proclaim his loss for one day.
But from the time that the Temple was destroyed,
may it be rebuilt speedily in our days,

phrases.aramaic: הִתְקִינוּ שֶׁיְּהוּ מַכְרִיזִים בְּבָתֵּי כְנֵסִיּוֹת וּבְבָתֵּי מִדְרָשׁוֹת.
phrases.english: the Sages instituted that those who find lost items shall proclaim their finds in synagogues and study halls.

phrases.aramaic: וּמִשֶּׁרַבּוּ הָאַנָּסִים הִתְקִינוּ שֶׁיְּהוּ מוֹדִיעִין לִשְׁכֵינָיו וְלִמְיוּדָּעָיו וְדַיּוֹ.
phrases.english: And from the time that the oppressors proliferated, the Sages instituted an ordinance that one who finds a lost item shall inform his neighbors and acquaintances, and that will suffice for him.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּנוּ רַבָּנַן: בָּרִאשׁוֹנָה, כָּל מִי שֶׁמָּצָא אֲבֵידָה

steps.title: Clarifying the meaning of 'bandits'
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks what is meant by 'from the time that the bandits proliferated'.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא seeks to clarify the specific threat posed by the 'bandits'. This is a direct question seeking definition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks what the term 'אַנָּסִים' refers to in this context. It answers that it refers to those who claim that lost items belong to the king. This explains why public proclamation became dangerous: it would lead to confiscation by the authorities.

keyTerms.meaning: A lost item belongs to the king

steps.whatToRemember: The 'bandits' are oppressive rulers who confiscate found items for the royal treasury.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'אַנָּסִים' is clarified to mean a specific type of oppressive authority.
steps.whyThisMatters: This clarifies the historical context for the change in halachic practice.

phrases.english: What is the meaning of: From the time that the oppressors proliferated?
It is from the time that they say: A lost item belongs to the king.
Rabbi Ami found a vessel full of dinars.
A certain Roman saw that he was wary and hesitant to take it.
The Roman said to him: Go, take it for yourself;

phrases.aramaic: דְּלָאו פָּרְסָאֵי אֲנַן דְּאָמְרִי אֲבֵידְתָּא לְמַלְכָּא.
phrases.english: as we are not Persians, who say that a lost item belongs to the king.

rashi.hebrew: אודיא דדינרי - כלי מלא זהובים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אודיא דדינרי refers to a vessel filled with gold coins.

steps.title: The Baraita on the Claimant's Stone in Jerusalem
steps.stepSummary: A Baraita describes the Claimant's Stone in Jerusalem where finders and owners would meet.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא concludes with a ברייתא describing a central location for resolving lost item claims. This is a setup line providing a concrete example.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא states that there was a stone in Jerusalem called the 'Claimant's Stone'. Finders would go there to proclaim their finds, and owners would go there to identify and claim their items. This provides a concrete example of how the proclamation system functioned in practice during the Temple period.
steps.whatToRemember: The 'אֶבֶן טוֹעִין' was a central hub for lost and found items in Jerusalem.
steps.confusionAlert: This ברייתא connects back to the משנה in תענית about the stone being obscured by water.
steps.whyThisMatters: It illustrates the practical implementation of the proclamation obligation in the Temple era.

phrases.aramaic: תָּנוּ רַבָּנַן: אֶבֶן טוֹעִ[י]ן הָיְתָה בִּירוּשָׁלַיִם.
phrases.english: The Sages taught in a baraita: There was a Claimant’s Stone in Jerusalem,

phrases.aramaic: כׇּל מִי שֶׁאָבְדָה לוֹ אֲבֵידָה נִפְנֶה לְשָׁם,
phrases.english: and anyone who lost an item would be directed there,

phrases.aramaic: וְכׇל מִי שֶׁמּוֹצֵא אֲבֵידָה נִפְנֶה לְשָׁם.
phrases.english: and anyone who found a lost item would be directed there.

phrases.aramaic: זֶה עוֹמֵד וּמַכְרִיז, וְזֶה עוֹמֵד וְנוֹתֵן סִימָנִין וְנוֹטְלָהּ.
phrases.english: This finder would stand and proclaim his find and that owner would stand and provide its distinguishing marks and take the item.
And that is the place about which we learned in a mishna (Ta’anit 19a):

phrases.aramaic: צְאוּ וּרְאוּ אִם נִמְחֵת אֶבֶן הַטּוֹעִ[י]ן.
phrases.english: Go and see if the Claimant’s Stone has been obscured by the rising water.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּנוּ רַבָּנַן: אֶבֶן טוֹעִין הָיְתָה בִּירוּשָׁלַיִם

rashi.hebrew: וזו היא ששנינו - במסכת תענית (דף יט.) בחוני המעגל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishnah referenced here is found in מסכת תענית regarding חוני המעגל.

rashi.hebrew: צאו וראו אם נמחת אבן טוען - אם נכסת בגשמים שגבהו הגשמים למעלה הימנו ויש גשמים יותר מדאי ואתפלל עליהם שילכו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that 'נמחת אבן טוען' refers to the stone being covered by excessive rain, which חוני המעגל would pray to remove.

tosafot.hebrew: אבן טוען - מפרש בירושלמי כשם שהאבן אינה יכולה למחות כך אין יכולים להתפלל על רוב טובה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות cites the ירושלמי to explain that just as the stone cannot object, one cannot pray for excessive abundance.

steps.title: The Mishna's rule on simanim and the רמאי
steps.stepSummary: The Mishna states that a claimant who identifies the item but not its distinguishing marks (סִימָנִין) does not receive it, and a fraudulent claimant (רמאי) does not receive it even with simanim, based on the verse requiring scrutiny.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with the משנה establishing the baseline rules for returning a lost item. This is a setup line introducing the core legal framework.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The משנה sets up two distinct categories of claimants: the honest one who fails to provide simanim, and the dishonest one who provides simanim but is still denied. The verse 'עַד דְּרֹשׁ אָחִיךָ' is interpreted as a command to scrutinize the claimant's honesty.

keyTerms.meaning: The verse requiring scrutiny of the claimant

steps.whatToRemember: The משנה distinguishes between a lack of simanim and the status of being a רמאי.
steps.confusionAlert: The verse 'עַד דְּרֹשׁ' is read as 'until you scrutinize your brother', not 'until your brother claims'.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the legal framework for the גמרא's discussion on how to identify and handle fraudulent claimants.

phrases.english: MISHNA: If a claimant accurately stated what type of item the lost item that was found by another is,
but did not state, i.e., describe, its distinguishing marks,
the finder shall not give it to him. And in the case of a swindler,
even though he stated its distinguishing marks,
the finder shall not give the lost item to him, as it is stated:

“And if your brother be not near you, and you know him not, then you shall bring it into your house, and it shall be with you until your brother claims [derosh] it [oto], and you shall return it to him” (Deuteronomy 22:2).

phrases.aramaic: עַד שֶׁתִּדְרוֹשׁ אֶת אָחִיךָ אִם רַמַּאי
phrases.english: You shall not return the lost item until you scrutinize [shetidrosh] your brother to determine whether he, the claimant, is a swindler
or whether he is not a swindler.

steps.title: The dispute between Rav Yehuda and Rav Nachman on the announcement
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces a dispute between Rav Yehuda and Rav Nachman regarding whether a finder should announce 'lost item' (אֲבֵידְתָּא) or the specific type of item (גְּלִימָא).
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a dispute between Rav Yehuda and Rav Nachman regarding the proper wording for the public announcement of a lost item. This is a setup line establishing the central halachic question of the sugya.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rav Yehuda rules the announcement should be vague ('אֲבֵידְתָּא'), while Rav Nachman rules it should be specific ('גְּלִימָא'). This debate centers on balancing the need to identify the true owner against the risk of enabling a fraudster.
steps.whatToRemember: Rav Yehuda says 'אֲבֵידְתָּא', Rav Nachman says 'גְּלִימָא'.
steps.whyThisMatters: This dispute drives the entire sugya's analysis of the Mishna's wording and the laws of the רמאי.

phrases.english: GEMARA: It was stated that Rav Yehuda said:
"A lost item" he proclaims, and Rav Naḥman said: "A cloak" he proclaims.

steps.title: Rav Yehuda's reasoning: fear of the רמאי
steps.stepSummary: Rav Yehuda explains that announcing 'lost item' prevents a swindler from guessing the item type and claiming it.
steps.whatsHappening: Rav Yehuda provides the rationale for his view: specificity helps the fraudster.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The concern is that if the finder announces 'garment', a swindler who knows someone lost a garment can easily guess the type and then fabricate simanim. Vagueness protects the honest owner.

keyTerms.meaning: We are concerned about a swindler

steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the security concern behind Rav Yehuda's ruling.

phrases.english: Rav Yehuda said: One who finds an item proclaims that he found a lost item,
as if you say that he proclaims that he found a cloak, we are concerned about the possibility that a swindler may attempt to claim the item.

rashi.hebrew: חיישינן לרמאי - שמא שמע האובד מתאונן בין שכיניו טליתי נאבדה וזה יודע את סימניה וכשישמע את מוצאה מכריז טלית מצאתי יקום ויתן סימניה הלכך אבידתא מכריז ואינו נותן לב לומר טלית זו אבדתי ואלו סימניה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern is that the owner might have complained to neighbors about losing a טלית, allowing a fraudster to hear the announcement, learn the simanim, and falsely claim it.

steps.title: Rav Nachman's reasoning: no end to the matter
steps.stepSummary: Rav Nachman explains that announcing the specific item is necessary because we don't worry about swindlers, as otherwise there would be no end to the problem.
steps.whatsHappening: Rav Nachman provides his rationale: the concern for swindlers is not practical.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rav Nachman argues that even with a vague announcement, a swindler could still guess the item type. Therefore, the concern for swindlers is unfounded, and specificity should be allowed to help the true owner.

keyTerms.meaning: There is no end to the matter

steps.whatToRemember: Rav Nachman's principle: אִם כֵּן אֵין לַדָּבָר סוֹף.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the practical concern behind Rav Nachman's ruling.

phrases.english: Rav Naḥman said: The finder proclaims that he found a cloak,
and we are not concerned about the possibility that a swindler may attempt to claim the item,
as if so, there is no end to the matter.

rashi.hebrew: אין לדבר סוף - דהשתא נמי מסיק אדעתיה ואמר אם טלית היא שמצאת אלו סימניה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern for swindlers is unfounded because even with a general announcement, a swindler could still claim the item by stating the specific signs of a garment they found.

steps.title: Challenge from the Mishna's wording
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges Rav Nachman's view using the Mishna: if the finder announces 'garment' and the claimant says 'garment', why does the Mishna need to state that without simanim the item is not returned?
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara attacks Rav Nachman's view by pointing out that the Mishna's wording seems redundant if Rav Nachman is right.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If Rav Nachman is correct and the finder announces 'garment', then when the claimant also says 'garment', it should be obvious that simanim are still required. The Mishna's explicit statement that 'if he did not state simanim, he does not receive it' seems unnecessary. This redundancy suggests Rav Nachman is wrong.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the redundancy of the Mishna's statement under Rav Nachman's view.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a classic redundancy attack: 'למה לי למיתנא X'.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces a resolution to the dispute between Rav Yehuda and Rav Nachman.

phrases.english: The Gemara cites proof from that which we learned in the mishna:

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר אֶת הָאֲבֵידָה וְלֹא אָמַר אֶת סִימָנֶיהָ –
phrases.english: If a claimant accurately states what type of item the lost item that was found by another is, but did not state its distinguishing marks,
the finder shall not give it to him.
Granted, if you say the finder proclaims that he found an unspecified lost item,

phrases.aramaic: הָא קָא מַשְׁמַע לַן אַף עַל
phrases.english: this mishna teaches us that even though
the claimant indeed stated that the lost item is a cloak,

phrases.aramaic: כִּי לָא אָמַר סִימָנִין לָא מַהְדְּרִינַן לֵיהּ.
phrases.english: as long as he did not state its distinguishing marks, we do not return it to him.
But if you say that the finder proclaims that he found a cloak,
if the finder stated that he found a cloak
and the claimant stated that he lost a cloak,

phrases.aramaic: צְרִיכָא לְמֵימַר כִּי לָא אָמַר סִימָנִין
phrases.english: does it need to be said that when he did not state its distinguishing marks
we do not return it to him?

rashi.hebrew: אי אמרת בשלמא אבידתא מכריז - ולא מזכיר שם החפץ אצטריך תנא לאשמועינן דאע"ג דאמר האי טלית אבדתי צריך ליתן סימנין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that if the finder announces only 'אבידתא' without specifying the item, the Mishna's requirement to state סימנים is necessary to teach that even if the claimant identifies the lost item as a טלית, he still must provide סימנים.

steps.title: Rav Safra's resolution: the Mishna refers to unclear simanim
steps.stepSummary: Rav Safra resolves the challenge by explaining that the Mishna refers to a case where the claimant provided simanim, but they were not clear-cut (מוּבְהָקִין).
steps.whatsHappening: Rav Safra provides a resolution that saves Rav Nachman's view.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rav Safra reinterprets the Mishna's phrase 'לֹא אָמַר אֶת סִימָנֶיהָ' to mean 'did not state clear-cut simanim'. This means the Mishna is teaching that even if the claimant provides some simanim, if they are not definitive, the item is not returned. This makes the Mishna's statement necessary even under Rav Nachman's view.
steps.whatToRemember: The resolution hinges on the definition of 'סִימָנִין מוּבְהָקִין'.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a classic 'הוה אמינא' resolution: the Mishna is needed to prevent a misunderstanding.
steps.whyThisMatters: This resolves the dispute in favor of Rav Nachman, at least regarding the Mishna's wording.

phrases.english: Rav Safra said:
Actually, one could say that the finder proclaims that he found a cloak,
and the mishna is referring to a case where the finder stated that he found a cloak,
and the claimant stated its distinguishing marks.
And what is the meaning of the phrase in the mishna: If he did not state its distinguishing marks?
It means: If he did not state its clear-cut distinguishing marks but rather stated distinguishing marks that are not exclusive to the item.

steps.title: The Britha on the evolution of the law for the רמאי
steps.stepSummary: A Britha explains that initially, simanim were sufficient, but when swindlers proliferated, the Sages instituted that witnesses must testify that the claimant is not a swindler.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a Britha that explains the historical development of the law regarding swindlers.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Britha shows that the requirement for witnesses is a later enactment (תקנה) in response to an increase in fraud. This provides the legal basis for the Mishna's rule that a רמאי does not receive the item even with simanim.
steps.whatToRemember: The requirement for witnesses is a תקנה due to the proliferation of רמאים.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the legal mechanism for dealing with swindlers.

phrases.aramaic: וְהָרַמַּאי אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁאָמַר אֶת סִימָנֶיהָ –
phrases.english: And in the case of a swindler, even though he stated its distinguishing marks,
the finder shall not give the lost item to him.
The Sages taught: Initially,
anyone who lost an item

phrases.aramaic: הָיָה נוֹתֵן סִימָנִין וְנוֹטְלָהּ. מִשֶּׁרַבּוּ הָרַמָּאִין,
phrases.english: would provide its distinguishing marks and take it. But when the swindlers proliferated,
the Sages instituted an ordinance that the finders will say to him:

phrases.aramaic: צֵא וְהָבֵא עֵדִים דְּלָאו רַמַּאי אַתְּ, וְטוֹל.
phrases.english: Go and bring witnesses who can testify that you are not a swindler, and take your item.

steps.title: The incident of Rav Pappa's father and the misunderstanding
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara relates an incident where Rav Pappa's father brought witnesses to prove he was not a swindler, but the witnesses misunderstood the question and testified that he was a swindler.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara illustrates the law with a story that highlights the potential for misunderstanding in the witness testimony.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The story shows the practical difficulty of the enactment: the witnesses misunderstood the question 'Do you know he is a swindler?' as 'Do you know he is not a swindler?' and answered 'Yes'. This creates a comedic but legally significant situation.

keyTerms.meaning: Do you know about him that he is a swindler?

steps.whatToRemember: The witnesses misunderstood the question due to the phrasing.
steps.confusionAlert: The witnesses thought the question was 'לָאו רַמַּאי אַתְּ' and answered affirmatively.
steps.whyThisMatters: This story illustrates the practical challenges of the enactment and sets up the final resolution.

phrases.aramaic: כִּי הָא דַּאֲבוּהּ דְּרַב פָּפָּא אִירְכַס
phrases.english: This is as in that incident involving the father of Rav Pappa, who lost a donkey
and others found it.

phrases.aramaic: אֲתָא לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַבָּה בַּר רַב הוּנָא, אֲמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: He came before Rabba bar Rav Huna, he said to him:

phrases.aramaic: זִיל אַיְיתִי סָהֲדִי דְּלָאו רַמַּאי אַתְּ,
phrases.english: Go and bring witnesses who can testify that you are not a swindler,

phrases.aramaic: וְטוֹל. אֲזַל אַיְיתִי סָהֲדֵי, אֲמַר לְהוּ:
phrases.english: and you may take your donkey. He went and brought witnesses, he said to them:
Do you know about him that he is a swindler?
They said to him: Yes.
He said to them: I am a swindler?!

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַרוּ לֵיהּ: אֲנַן ״לָאו רַמַּאי אַתְּ״ קָאָמְרִינַן.
phrases.english: They said to him: We were saying that you are not a swindler.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבָּה בַּר רַב הוּנָא: מִסְתַּבְּרָא,
phrases.english: Rabba bar Rav Huna said: It is reasonable to conclude,
a person does not bring condemnation upon himself.

rashi.hebrew: אמרו ליה אין - כסבורין שאמר להן ידעיתון ביה דלאו רמאי הוא שכך היה לו לשואלן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the witnesses answered 'yes' because they misunderstood the question as asking if they knew he was NOT a swindler, which was the proper way to ask.

rashi.hebrew: מסתברא - שזה היה דעתן מתחילה ואין כאן משום מגיד וחוזר ומגיד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the witnesses' initial intent was to testify truthfully, so there is no issue of מגיד וחוזר ומגיד since they didn't retract their testimony.

rashi.hebrew: לא מייתי איניש חובה לנפשיה - וכשהביאן לב"ד בטוח היה שלא יעידו חובתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבָּה בר רב הונא's statement means a person does not bring witnesses to court expecting them to testify against him, so the witnesses assumed the question was phrased correctly.

steps.title: Duration of care for productive animals
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara establishes that the obligation to care for a productive animal lasts for twelve months, citing Rav Naḥman quoting Shmuel and a supporting baraita.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces the duration of the obligation to care for a lost animal that works and eats, citing Rav Naḥman quoting Shmuel and a baraita.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna states the general rule that a productive animal should work and eat, but does not specify a time limit. The Gemara fills this gap by citing Rav Naḥman quoting Shmuel that the obligation lasts twelve months. This is supported by a baraita which explicitly states the twelve-month period for animals like cows and donkeys.

keyTerms.meaning: assesses their value and sets them aside

steps.whatToRemember: The obligation to care for a productive animal is limited to twelve months.
steps.confusionAlert: The Mishna's silence on the duration is filled by Amoraic tradition and a baraita.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes a practical time limit for the mitzvah of returning a lost item, preventing an indefinite burden on the finder.

phrases.english: MISHNA: If one finds any living being that works and generates enough revenue to cover the costs of the food that it eats,

phrases.aramaic: יַעֲשֶׂה וְיֹאכַל, וְדָבָר שֶׁאֵין עוֹשֶׂה וְאוֹכֵל –
phrases.english: it shall work and eat while in the finder’s possession. And any living being that does not work but it does eat shall be sold,
as it is stated: “Then you shall bring it into your house, and it shall be with you until your brother claims it, and you shall return it to him” (Deuteronomy 22:2),
indicating that the finder must see how best to return it to him.
What shall be done with the money received from the sale of the animal?
Rabbi Tarfon says: The finder may use it;
therefore, if the money is lost,
he is liable to pay restitution for it. Rabbi Akiva says:
He may not use the money;
therefore, if it is lost,

he is not liable to pay restitution for it. GEMARA: The mishna teaches that an animal that generates enough revenue to cover the costs of the food that it eats shall work and eat while in the finder’s possession. The Gemara asks: And must he care for the animal forever?
Rav Naḥman says that Shmuel says:
He cares for the animal until twelve months pass.
This is also taught in a baraita:

phrases.aramaic: כׇּל דָּבָר שֶׁעוֹשֶׂה וְאוֹכֵל כְּגוֹן פָּרָה וַחֲמוֹר –
phrases.english: If one finds any living being that works and generates enough revenue to cover the costs of the food that it eats, e.g., a cow or a donkey,

phrases.aramaic: מִטַּפֵּל בָּהֶן עַד שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר חֹדֶשׁ,
phrases.english: he tends to them until twelve months pass.
From that point forward,
one assesses their value, sells them, and places the money aside for the owner.

rashi.hebrew: מתני' כל דבר שעושה ואוכל - אם אבידה זו דבר שיכולין להאכילו את שכר מעשיו כגון שור וחמור יעשה ויאכל ולא ימכור אותו המוצא אם שהו בעליו מלדרשו שכל אדם נוח לו בבהמתו שהכירה בו כבר ולימדה לרצונו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishna's rule to let a productive animal work and eat applies only when the owner delays claiming it, as the owner prefers an animal already trained to his will.

rashi.hebrew: ראה היאך תשיבנו לו - שלא תאכילנו חצי דמיו שא"כ אין זו השבה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the verse 'והשבותו לו' implies returning the animal in its original condition, prohibiting the finder from feeding it only half its value.

rashi.hebrew: גמ' ולעולם - וכי עד עולם זקוק זה לטפל בטורח שמירה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the Gemara questions whether the obligation to care for the animal lasts forever, given the burden of guarding it.

rashi.hebrew: שם דמיהן - מוכרן ומניח הדמים אצלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that 'שם דמיהן' means the finder sells the animal and keeps the money.

tosafot.hebrew: שם דמיהן - לקמן גבי עסקא דרב ספרא אפרש בע"ה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות notes that the concept of 'שם דמיהן' will be explained later regarding the case of רב ספרא's business arrangement.

steps.title: Duration of care for young animals and poultry
steps.stepSummary: The baraita continues, specifying shorter durations for calves, foals, geese, and roosters, and Rav Naḥman bar Yitzḥak adds a ruling about chickens.
steps.whatsHappening: The ברייתא details time limits for caring for calves, foals, geese, and roosters. רַב נַחְמָן בַּר יִצְחָק adds a ruling about chickens.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The first source sets three months for calves and foals, and thirty days for geese and roosters. רַב נַחְמָן בַּר יִצְחָק rules that a chicken requires twelve months of care, like a large domesticated animal. A second ברייתא is cited with conflicting durations for the same animals.

phrases.english: If one finds calves and foals
he tends to them for three months
From that point forward
one assesses their value, sells them, and places the money aside for the owner
If one finds geese and roosters
he tends to them for thirty days
From that point forward
one assesses their value, sells them, and places the money aside for the owner

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן בַּר יִצְחָק: תַּרְנְגוֹלֶת כִּבְהֵמָה גַּסָּה.
phrases.english: Rav Naḥman bar Yitzḥak says: The legal status of a chicken is like that of a large domesticated animal
This is also taught in a baraita:
If one finds a chicken and a large domesticated animal
he tends to them for twelve months
From that point forward
one assesses their value, sells them, and places the money aside for the owner
If one finds calves and foals
he tends to them for thirty days
From that point forward
one assesses their value, sells them, and places the money aside for the owner

phrases.aramaic: אֲווֹזִין וְתַרְנְגוֹלִין וְכׇל דָּבָר שֶׁטִּיפּוּלוֹ מְרוּבֶּה מִשְּׂכָרוֹ –
phrases.english: If one finds geese and roosters and anything that costs more to tend to than the revenue generated by it
he tends to them for three days
From that point forward
one assesses their value, sells them, and places the money aside for the owner

rashi.hebrew: עגלים וסייחין - דקין שאין עושין מלאכה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that עגלים וסייחין refers to young animals that are not yet strong enough to perform labor.

rashi.hebrew: תרנגולין - זכרים שאין מטילין בצים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that תרנגולין refers specifically to male chickens that do not lay eggs.

rashi.hebrew: כבהמה גסה - שעושה ואוכלת ומטפל בה י"ב חדש דיכול להאכילה דמי בצים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that a תרנגולת is treated like a בהמה גסה because it works and eats, justifying twelve months of care during which the owner can feed it from the value of the eggs it lays.

rashi.hebrew: מטפל - אנשמיטר"א בלע"ז:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the term מטפל is a foreign word (Ansheimter in the vernacular) meaning to care for or tend to.

steps.title: Contradiction between the two baraitot
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara points out the contradiction between the two baraitot regarding the duration of care for calves, foals, geese, and roosters.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא identifies a direct contradiction between the two ברייתות regarding time limits.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The first source says three months for calves/foals and thirty days for geese/roosters. The second source says thirty days for calves/foals and three days for geese/roosters. The גמרא highlights this discrepancy as a קַשְׁיָא.

keyTerms.meaning: it is difficult / there is a contradiction

phrases.english: It is difficult, as there is a contradiction between the ruling in the first baraita that the finder keeps calves and foals for three months and the ruling in the second baraita that the finder keeps calves and foals for thirty days

and there is another contradiction between the ruling in the first baraita that the finder keeps geese and roosters for thirty days, and the ruling in the second baraita that the finder keeps geese and roosters for three days

steps.title: Resolution: different types of calves and foals
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the contradiction regarding calves and foals by distinguishing between those that graze and those that need fattening.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the first part of the contradiction by distinguishing between two types of calves and foals.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The first ברייתא refers to calves and foals that graze in the pasture, which are easier to maintain, hence the longer period of three months. The second ברייתא refers to those that need to be fattened, which require more effort, hence the shorter period of thirty days.

phrases.aramaic: עֲגָלִים וּסְיָיחִין אַעֲגָלִים וּסְיָיחִין לָא קַשְׁיָא:
phrases.english: The Gemara answers: The contradiction between the ruling in the first baraita with regard to calves and foals and the ruling in the second baraita with regard to calves and foals is not difficult.

This ruling in the first baraita that the finder keeps them for three months is referring to calves and foals that graze in the pasture, and that ruling in the second baraita that the finder keeps them for thirty days is referring to calves and foals that need to be fattened and therefore require greater exertion on the part of the one who finds them.

steps.triggerLanguage: עֲגָלִים וּסְיָיחִין אַעֲגָלִים וּסְיָיחִין לָא קַשְׁיָא

rashi.hebrew: הא דרעיא הא דפטומא - הא דקתני שלשה [חדשים] בארץ מרעה ובזמן הדשאים שאין טיפולו מרובה והא דקתני ל' [יום] בזמן שאין מרעה וצריך לפטמה על אבוסה ממה שבבית שדמיה יקרים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the three-month period applies to calves and foals grazing in pastures during the season of abundant grass when care is minimal, while the thirty-day period applies when there is no pasture and they must be fattened on expensive feed from the house.

steps.title: Resolution: different sizes of geese and roosters
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the contradiction regarding geese and roosters by distinguishing between large and small ones.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the second part of the contradiction by distinguishing between large and small geese and roosters.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The first ברייתא refers to large geese and roosters, which are easier to maintain, hence the longer period of thirty days. The second ברייתא refers to small ones, which require more effort, hence the shorter period of three days.

phrases.aramaic: אֲווֹזִין וְתַרְנְגוֹלִין אַאֲווֹזִין וְתַרְנְגוֹלִין נָמֵי לָא קַשְׁיָא:
phrases.english: The contradiction between the ruling in the first baraita with regard to geese and roosters and the ruling in the second baraita with regard to geese and roosters is also not difficult.

This ruling in the first baraita that the finder keeps them for thirty days is referring to large geese and roosters, which do not require great exertion, and that ruling in the second baraita that the finder keeps them for three days is referring to small geese and roosters, which require great exertion.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲווֹזִין וְתַרְנְגוֹלִין אַאֲווֹזִין וְתַרְנְגוֹלִין נָמֵי לָא קַשְׁיָא

rashi.hebrew: רברבי - אוכלין הרבה הלכך ג' ימים דהא אוקימנא בזכרים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רברבי refers to large geese and roosters that eat a lot, which is why the three-day period applies to them, as the Gemara has already established that the discussion concerns males.

steps.title: Prohibition on feeding young animals with their own value
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites a baraita explaining that one should not feed a calf to calves, etc., to avoid depleting the value of the lost item.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites a ברייתא explaining the principle behind selling non-productive animals.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא explains that one should not use the value of the lost item to maintain it if it will deplete the item's value. This prevents the finder from inadvertently causing a loss to the owner.

keyTerms.meaning: not to feed a calf to calves

phrases.english: The mishna teaches: And any living being that does not work but it does eat shall be sold. The Sages taught in a baraita:
The verse states: “And you shall return it to him” (Deuteronomy 22:2),
indicating that the finder must see how best to return it to him,
meaning that one shall not feed the value of a calf to the lost calves that he is tending,

phrases.aramaic: וּסְיָח לִסְיָיחִין, אֲווֹזָא לַאֲווֹזִין, וְתַרְנְגוֹל לְתַרְנְגוֹלִין.
phrases.english: nor the value of a foal to the lost foals that he is tending, nor the value of a goose to the geese that he is tending, nor the value of a rooster to the roosters that he is tending.

rashi.hebrew: שלא יאכיל עגל לעגלים - מן העגלים שנמצא לא יאכיל לו דמי עגל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the prohibition of feeding a calf to calves means one may not use the proceeds from selling a found calf to feed other calves.

steps.title: Agreement on liability when money is not used
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara states that Rabbi Tarfon and Rabbi Akiva agree that if the finder does not use the money, he is exempt from liability if it is lost.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא analyzes the dispute between רַבִּי טַרְפוֹן and רַבִּי עֲקִיבָה, establishing a point of agreement.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The מִשְׁנָה presents a dispute about liability for lost money. The גמרא clarifies that the dispute only applies when the finder uses the money. If he does not use it, both agree he is exempt from liability.

phrases.english: What shall be done with the money received from the sale of the animal?
Rabbi Tarfon says: The finder may use it; therefore, if the money is lost, he is liable to pay restitution for its loss.

phrases.aramaic: עַד כָּאן לָא פְּלִיגִי אֶלָּא כְּשֶׁנִּשְׁתַּמֵּשׁ בָּהֶן.
phrases.english: Rabbi Tarfon and Rabbi Akiva disagree only in a case where the finder used the money.

phrases.aramaic: אֲבָל לֹא נִשְׁתַּמֵּשׁ בָּהֶן, אִם אָבְדוּ – פָּטוּר.
phrases.english: But in a case where the finder did not use the money, everyone agrees that if the money is lost, the finder is exempt from paying restitution for its loss.

rashi.hebrew: עד כאן לא פליגי - לעניין להתחייב באבידתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's statement 'עד כאן לא פליגי' refers specifically to the point of agreement regarding liability for the loss of the money.

tosafot.hebrew: עד כאן לא פליגי אלא בנשתמש בהן - לאו דוקא נשתמש דאם נשתמש לר"ע נמי חייב באחריותן כיון דלדידיה אסור להשתמש א"כ הוי שולח יד בפקדון ואפילו החזיר אין בכך כלום דהא ר"ע סבר בפרק המפקיד (לקמן בבא מציעא דף מג:) דבעינן דעת בעלים אלא ודאי נשתמש לאו דוקא כדפרישית אלא בשכר שמוש שמותר להשתמש קאמר דלרבי טרפון דאית ליה דמותר להשתמש חייב באחריותן דבההוא הנאה דאי מיתרמי ליה זבינא שקיל להו וזבין בהו הוי עלייהו שומר שכר ולר"ע דאסור להשתמש בהן פטור אבל אם לא היה מותר להשתמש היה פטור לכ"ע והיינו כרבה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that 'נשתמש' does not mean actual physical use, which would make one liable as a thief according to רבי עקיבא, but rather refers to the 'שכר שמוש' (benefit of use) that transforms the status to שומר שכר according to רבי טרפון, whereas if use were forbidden, all would agree on exemption.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות challenges a literal reading of 'נשתמש' that רש״י might imply, clarifying that the term refers to the theoretical benefit of use (שכר שמוש) rather than actual usage, which would trigger different halachic categories.
